justification: All pass criteria met: (1) All 4 business_impact sub-fields (financial, operational, user, risk) populated with substantive multi-sentence analysis. (2) stakeholders_affected lists 8 distinct groups with specific impact per group, far exceeding the 3 minimum. (3) 4 escalations for missing information, all well-structured. (4) Contractual SLA risk explicitly identified in both risk field and BR-5. (5) confidence.level at 45, well under 80 threshold, with detailed assumptions listed. (6) Competitive positioning explicitly mentioned in risk field ('competitors offering time-level ETAs will appear more reliable') and in product/marketing stakeholder impact.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough stakeholder analysis with 8 distinct groups, each with specific, actionable impact descriptions
Confidence level of 45 is well-calibrated — appropriately low given missing contract information, motivation, and product portfolio details
4 high-quality escalations covering motivation, tier segmentation, operational definition of 'by Friday', and API versioning — all with structured options and recommendations
Contractual SLA risk addressed both as a business rule (BR-5) and in acceptance criteria (AC-4), showing it was treated as a first-class concern
Correctly identified the operational benefit (wider windows = more flexibility) while balancing against customer experience costs and metric gaming risk
Edge cases are excellent — same-day delivery, premium tier erosion, API breaking changes, timezone boundaries, and support agent scripting all identified
Financial analysis covers both sides: reduced refund payouts vs. conversion rate drops and premium churn
The escalation about motivation ('WHY is this change being made?') is a sophisticated domain insight — the fix might be better predictions, not hiding precision

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) All 4 business_impact sub-fields (financial, operational, user, risk) contain substantive multi-sentence analysis. (2) stakeholders_affected lists 8 distinct groups with specific impact per group, far exceeding the 3 minimum. (3) Four detailed escalations for missing information (product line scope, B2B SLA contracts, API vs UI scope, timezone/EOD definition). (4) Contractual SLA risk explicitly identified in multiple places (BR-4, business_impact.risk, escalation #2). (5) confidence.level is 52, well under 80, with detailed assumptions listed. (6) Competitive positioning mentioned in business_impact.user ('competitors offering tighter ETAs will have a differentiation advantage').

observations.text: Exceptional stakeholder breakdown — 8 distinct groups including often-missed ones like API consumers, analytics teams, and sales/account management
Confidence level of 52 is well-calibrated given the missing contract and product-line information
Identified same-day delivery incoherence as a critical edge case — a non-obvious domain insight
BR-3 (SLA measurement alignment) and BR-4 (contractual SLA preservation) show deep domain reasoning beyond surface-level analysis
Flagged the risk of artificially inflated on-time delivery rates masking operational degradation — a subtle second-order effect
Four well-structured escalations with options and recommendations, covering contractual, product-line, API, and timezone ambiguities
Competitive positioning risk explicitly called out in the user impact section
Edge cases section covers API breaking change, midnight boundary, and historical data integrity — all operationally relevant

trials.justification: All 4 business_impact sub-fields (financial, operational, user, risk) are substantively populated with multi-sentence analysis. stakeholders_affected lists 7 distinct groups with specific impact per group. 3 escalations raise missing information that cannot be assumed. Contractual SLA risk is explicitly and repeatedly identified (BR-2, escalation 1, risk section). confidence.level is 45, well below the 80 threshold, with detailed assumptions listed. Competitive positioning is mentioned in the risk field ('competitors offering precise ETAs gain an advantage').

observations.text: Exceptional stakeholder breakdown with 7 distinct groups, each with specific, differentiated impact analysis — far exceeds the 3-group minimum.
Three well-structured escalations with options and recommendations, covering tier scope, prediction accuracy, and API impact.
Confidence level of 45 is well-calibrated — correctly identifies that without contract information and tier scope, the analysis is necessarily uncertain.
Identifies the premium tier devaluation risk as a financial impact, directly addressing revenue implications.
Goes beyond the rubric by including rejection_reasons with specific business rule violations, adding actionable guardrails.
Edge cases are practical and domain-aware (timezone boundaries, same-day exclusion, API versioning, perishables/medical regulatory requirements).
Correctly distinguishes between display-layer and internal precision concerns (BR-3), preventing a common implementation mistake.
